question: Will the highest temperature in London be 18°C on February 25?
description:
This market will resolve to the temperature range that contains the highest temperature recorded at the London City Airport Station in degrees Celsius on 25 Feb '26.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day by the Forecast for the London City Airport Station once information is finalized, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/gb/london/EGLC.

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the gear icon next to the search bar and switch the Temperature setting between °F and °C.

This market can not resolve to "Yes" until all data for this date has been finalized.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Any revisions to temperatures recorded after data is finalized for this market's timeframe will not be considered for this market's resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Official meteorological observations from Spain's State Meteorological Agency confirmed a maximum temperature of 20°C in Madrid on May 16, driving the market's near-certain 100% implied probability for this outcome. This reading aligns with mid-May climatological norms for the Iberian Peninsula, where spring warming patterns typically produce daily highs in the 18–22°C range under stable high-pressure systems. Forecast models from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts had projected minimal deviation from these baselines in the 48 hours prior, with no significant heat advection or urban heat-island amplification observed. While final official reports can occasionally undergo minor revisions for instrumentation checks, historical precedent shows such adjustments rarely exceed 1°C after initial publication, leaving little realistic scope for market-moving changes.

This positioning stems from a 3-5°C drop projected since April 27, easing prior heatwave intensity after peaks above 41°C earlier in the week, driven by stable high-pressure systems and reduced solar heating potential per model consensus. Resolution awaits finalized Wunderground history to whole-degree precision, though urban heat island effects or unexpected wind lulls could marginally challenge this if late-day readings nudge toward 40°C; current observations indicate negligible risk.

question: Will October 2021 average global temperature be the highest October temperature on record?
description:
This is a market on whether the average global land and ocean surface temperature for October 2021 will be the highest October temperature since global records began in 1880. The resolution source for this market will be the Global Climate Report for October 2021, published by NOAA's National Centers for Environmental Information (https://www.ncdc.noaa.gov/sotc/global/2021). This market will resolve to “Yes” if, for the month of October 2021 averaged as a whole, global land and ocean surface temperature anomaly, as measured by U.S. National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, will be ranked the highest for October since global records began, and “No” otherwise. Note that if this October ties for highest, it will result in the market resolving to "Yes." Past data for the month of October can be found here https://www.ncdc.noaa.gov/cag/global/time-series/globe/land_ocean/1/10/1880-2021. This market will resolve when data first becomes available for the month of October 2021.

Note: According to data at market's inception, the warmest October was in 2015, when the average global land and ocean temperature was 1.03°C (1.85°F) above the 20th century average of 14.0°C (57.1°F). However, that data might be modified, thus the market resolves to "Yes" only if NOAA lists October 2021 as the hottest or tied for the hottest since the records began.

question: Will September 2021 average global temperature be the highest September temperature on record?
description:
In Response to Trader Inquiry: At the time of this market’s inception, the record high for September was +0.95 °C above the 20th century average. This value will continue to be used as the benchmark for this market, i.e. if September 2021 reports a value of +0.95 °C or higher, the market will resolve to “Yes,” otherwise “No.” Retroactive modification of past values will have no bearing on this market’s resolution.

This is a market on whether the average global land and ocean surface temperature for September 2021 will be the highest September temperature since global records began in 1880.
The resolution source for this market will be the Global Climate Report for September 2021, published by NOAA's National Centers for Environmental Information (https://www.ncdc.noaa.gov/sotc/global/2021). 

This market will resolve to “Yes” if, for the month of September 2021 averaged as a whole, global land and ocean surface temperature anomaly, as measured by U.S. National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, will be equal to or greater than 0.95°C (1.71°F) above the 20th century average of 15.0°C (59.0°F), and “No” otherwise. Note that if this September ties for highest, it will result in the market resolving to "Yes."  Past data for the month of September can be found here https://www.ncdc.noaa.gov/cag/global/time-series/globe/land_ocean/1/9/1880-2021. This market will resolve when data is first available for the month of September 2021.

question: Will the high temperature in New York's Central Park be 60°F or higher on November 2, 2021?
description:
This market will resolve to “Yes”, if the maximum air temperature measured on November 2nd by the National Weather Service in Central Park Area, NY, is 60°F or higher, and ""No"" otherwise.

The resolution source for this market is NOWdata, published by National Weather Service, published on their website: https://www.weather.gov/wrh/Climate?wfo=okx.

The check will be at 12:00 PM ET November 3.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day by the Forecast for the Paris-Le Bourget Airport Station once information is finalized, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/fr/bonneuil-en-france/LFPB.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

eventMetadata.context_description: Singapore's equatorial location and the ongoing inter-monsoon period drive consistently elevated surface temperatures, with official observations from the National Environment Agency confirming a daily maximum of at least 33 °C on May 19. Strong solar insolation combined with typical light winds and urban heat retention in the city-state reliably pushes readings into this range during mid-May, aligning with climatological averages of 32–34 °C and recent model guidance showing minimal cooling influence from nearby sea breezes. Trader positioning at near-certainty for 33 °C or higher therefore mirrors verified meteorological data rather than forecast uncertainty. Only an unexpected surge in convective cloud cover or heavy afternoon rainfall could have suppressed the peak below this level, though such conditions remained absent in real-time satellite and surface reports.

Taiwan's Central Weather Administration records show typical daily highs of 27–30°C during this period, supported by strengthening southerly monsoon flows, elevated sea-surface temperatures, and minimal frontal activity that limits cooling. Recent forecast model runs have reinforced this baseline with only modest day-to-day variability expected, consistent with historical May patterns where temperatures frequently exceed 28°C. While an unexpected northerly surge or prolonged rainfall could temporarily suppress readings, such shifts remain statistically uncommon this late in the spring transition. Official station data will determine final resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Latest forecast model runs from agencies tracking the Red Sea region point to a daytime high in Jeddah today near 37–39 °C, driven by persistent high-pressure ridging and light westerly flow that limits cloud cover while allowing heat to build over the coastal plain. Sea-surface temperatures around 30 °C sustain elevated humidity, which can amplify apparent heat but also cap extreme peaks through marine-layer moderation. Model consensus remains tight, though small differences in boundary-layer mixing or afternoon sea-breeze timing explain why probabilities cluster between 36 °C and 40 °C rather than diverging sharply. Historical May climatology shows typical maxima of 35–38 °C, so any deviation above 40 °C would require an unusually strong offshore flow or delayed cloud formation—conditions not currently favored in the latest guidance.

question: Will August 2021 average global temperature be the highest August temperature on record?
description:
This is a market on whether the average global land and ocean surface temperature for August 2021 will be the highest August temperature since global records began in 1880.
The resolution source for this market will be the Global Climate Report for August 2021, published by NOAA's National Centers for Environmental Information (https://www.ncdc.noaa.gov/sotc/global/2021). 

This market will resolve to “Yes” if, for the month of August 2021 averaged as a whole, global land and ocean surface temperature anomaly, as measured by U.S. National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, will be greater than 0.98°C (1.76°F) above the 20th century average of 15.6°C (60.1°F), and “No” otherwise. Past data for the month of August can be found here https://www.ncdc.noaa.gov/cag/global/time-series/globe/land_ocean/1/8/1880-2021. This market will resolve when data is first available for the month of August 2021. In the event of an ambiguity in terms of the market outcome, the market will be resolved in good faith at the sole discretion of the Markets Integrity Committee (MIC).
